source_title: Longevity 101: a foundational guide to Peter's frameworks for longevity, and understanding CVD, cancer, neurodegenerative disease, nutrition, exercise, sleep, and more (re-broadcast)

paragraphs.speaker: peter
paragraphs.name: Peter Attia
paragraphs.text: Welcome to the drive podcast. I'm your host, Peter Attia. This podcast, my website, and my weekly newsletter all focus on the goal of translating the science of longevity into something accessible for everyone. Today's episode is different—it's longevity 101. We're going to do something that reads more like an AMA, answering foundational questions. This is for people who are new or who want to share a starting point with friends. We'll lay the foundation for how I think about longevity: the structure, the concepts of lifespan, healthspan, the four horsemen of death, marginal decade. We'll talk about the five tactics: exercise, nutrition, sleep, drugs and supplements, emotional health. Since this is a superficial treatment of these concepts, the show notes will point you in the deeper direction of anything covered. If you're feeling overwhelmed about some of our content, this episode gives foundational information that allows you to appreciate the deeper dives we're more commonly doing.

paragraphs.speaker: peter
paragraphs.name: Peter Attia
paragraphs.text: How do I define longevity? I don't make an argument that my definition is the best, but whenever someone talks about it, it's worth asking what they mean. Longevity is a function made up of two vectors: lifespan and healthspan. Lifespan is discrete, binary, and objective—you are alive or you are dead. It's death certificate death, respiring or not. Healthspan is far more complicated, subjective, and analog. It has three components: physical, cognitive, and emotional. Two of those inevitably decline with age—physical and cognitive. I will never again reach the physical or cognitive pinnacles I had in my late teens and 20s. But emotional health doesn't tie to age much at all. I can be emotionally better off in a decade than today, and I am certainly better off today than a decade ago. So longevity means how do we live longer—years longer, a decade longer, not a doubling of lifespan—and how do we reduce the rate of decline of healthspan.

paragraphs.speaker: peter
paragraphs.name: Peter Attia
paragraphs.text: Why focus on healthspan, not just lifespan? First, theoretically, anyone who thinks about it realizes any desire to live longer has to be accompanied by preserving healthspan. If you want to live to 95, you don't want to look like most 95-year-olds—you want to function like a healthy 75-year-old. Second, all the things you do to improve healthspan are twofers. You're getting two for one. When you do all those things to improve your strength, endurance, stamina, balance, coordination, processing speed, working memory, emotional health, happiness, relationships, you also improve your lifespan. I believe you would capture three-quarters of the way towards optimizing your lifespan just by pursuing better health, even if you never once thought about heart disease, cancer, Alzheimer's disease. Pursuit of healthspan is valuable in its own right, and it probably lengthens life to a greater effect than all the efforts medicine 2.0 puts directly into lifespan extension.

paragraphs.speaker: peter
paragraphs.name: Peter Attia
paragraphs.text: Medicine 1.0 dominated for virtually all of human existence—about 250,000 years until the late 19th century. It wasn't scientific in the way we understand science today. It relied on gods, spirits, humors, and was largely ineffective. Median life expectancy was late 30s or early 40s. Causes of death were communicable diseases, infections, child mortality, maternal mortality. Then, after the Civil War, Francis Bacon's scientific method became germane to medicine. You observe, form a hypothesis, design an experiment, conduct it, measure the outcome, compare results to the hypothesis prediction. Layer on the light microscope, germ theory, antimicrobial agents, sanitation—this led to Medicine 2.0. From the late 1800s, just 100 years later, human lifespan approximately doubled. Medicine 2.0 developed randomized controlled trials, and for infections, congestive heart failure, renal failure, appendicitis, complicated pregnancies, it's an enormous success. I wouldn't be here without it.

paragraphs.speaker: peter
paragraphs.name: Peter Attia
paragraphs.text: Why do we need Medicine 3.0? For all the successes of Medicine 2.0, it has notable failures. Lifespan has largely faltered beyond eradicating conditions that killed people 150 years ago. The leading causes of death today—the four horsemen—are atherosclerosis, cancer, neurodegenerative diseases like Alzheimer's, Parkinson's, Lewy body dementia, and metabolic diseases. COPD is also a major cause, but that's mostly cigarette smoking—a public health question. Medicine 3.0 is aimed at preventing rather than treating chronic disease by acting early, acting aggressively, and tailoring therapies to individuals based on the best available evidence, not necessarily randomized control trials. The second pillar of Medicine 3.0 is that healthspan is to be given at least as much effort and attention as lifespan. Medicine 2.0 does not place emphasis on healthspan. We need to shift resources away from solely focusing on Medicine 2.0 to focusing on Medicine 3.0. Medicine 3.0's job is to make encounters with Medicine 2.0 less frequent, less severe, and later in life.
